extra.reasoning:
The property G(opted_out implies not sent) — equivalently the invariant not(opted_out and sent) — holds on all reachable executions.

Inductive argument:
- Initial state: opted_out=false, sent=false. Invariant holds.
- The ONLY transition that sets sent:=true is t2 (send_attempt), whose guard is "not opted_out". So after t2, opted_out=false, making the antecedent false; invariant holds.
- The ONLY transition that sets opted_out:=true is t1 (optout), which simultaneously sets sent:=false. So after t1, opted_out=true and sent=false; invariant holds.
- t3 (send_attempt when opted_out) sets sent:=false → invariant holds.
- t4 (tick) sets sent:=false and never touches opted_out → invariant holds.

No transition ever sets opted_out back to false, so opted_out is monotone (once true, stays true). Crucially, the only way to make sent true (t2) is guarded by not opted_out, so it cannot fire once opted_out is true. And the act of setting opted_out true (t1) clears sent. Therefore the bad state (opted_out=true and sent=true) is unreachable. Every transition preserves the invariant, so it holds on every reachable state of every execution (including within the 12-event bound). Property holds; no counterexample exists.
